1	# Clean-room reimplementation protocol — tamarin-rs relicensing
2	
3	Purpose: independently recreate three modules of tamarin-rs (wellformedness
4	checker, graph/dot pipeline, web layer) without access to GPL-covered
5	expression, so the results are original works relicensable at will.
6	
7	## Rooms
8	- DIRTY ROOM (Kevin Milner + main-session assistant): has read the GPL Haskell
9	  source and the ported Rust modules. Produces only: interoperability headers
10	  (type surfaces, entry-point signatures, comments stripped), oracle wrappers,
11	  and behavioral bug reports. Never contributes implementation text.
12	- CLEAN ROOM (isolated agents, one per cluster): receive ONLY the materials
13	  listed per cluster below. Their complete tool-call transcripts are retained
14	  as evidence of non-access.
15	
16	## Clean-room materials (exhaustive)
17	1. This protocol, the cluster SPEC.md, and interface/ headers (type surfaces +
18	   boundary signatures only).
19	2. Black-box oracles: `wf_oracle.sh` (runs the compiled Haskell binary on an
20	   input file), `hs_server.sh` (serves a theory in the Haskell web UI for HTTP
21	   probing), pre-captured oracle outputs (oracle/captures/,
22	   oracle/captured_responses/), and GPL example .spthy files used solely as
23	   observation INPUTS.
24	3. Public Rust toolchain and general documentation.
25	
26	## Forbidden to the clean room
27	- Reading ANY file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/ — source, comments, git
28	  history — with the sole exceptions of executing the oracle scripts and
29	  reading oracle/captured_responses/ (captured program OUTPUT, not source).
30	- Fetching tamarin-prover source, docs describing its internals, or its GitHub.
31	- Reproducing any tamarin-prover implementation detail from model memory:
32	  every behavior must be derived from the oracle, the captures, or the SPEC.
33	  Exact output strings (warning texts, DOT syntax, HTML) MUST be taken from
34	  observed oracle output — that is compatibility content, not expression.
35	
36	## Audit & integration (after implementation)
37	1. Transcript audit: verify from the retained agent transcripts that no
38	   forbidden path was accessed.
39	2. Similarity audit: a reviewer (may see both sides) compares the clean code
40	   against the Haskell originals; flagged similarities are sent back as
41	   redo-instructions, never patched directly.
42	3. Behavioral acceptance: byte-parity corpus must pass.
43	4. Integration: dirty room may write thin adapters and mechanical renames to
44	   fit the workspace API, but must not transplant logic from the replaced
45	   modules. The replaced modules are deleted; their GPL-pending headers go
46	   with them (history retains them under GPL).

1 # BEHAVIOR.md — observed spec of the tamarin web-UI constraint-system graph payload 2 3 All statements below are derived from OBSERVED oracle output: the 81 captured 4 crawl manifests under `oracle/captured_responses/` (14705 graph payloads) and 5 targeted live probing of the black-box HS binary (see QUERIES.log). No 6 tamarin-prover source was read. Where a behavior could not be pinned down 7 black-box it is marked **[GAP]**. 8 9 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 10 ## 1. Transport / payload family 11 12 A crawl manifest is JSON: `{base, lemmas, log, capped, manifest}` where 13 `manifest` maps a URL to `{kind, status, body}` (kind ∈ html|text|json|dot). 14 15 Graph-bearing URL families (theory index `N` replaces `#` in captures): 16 17 | URL family | kind | payload | 18 |---|---|---| 19 | `/thy/trace/N/interactive-graph-def/proof/[/...]` | `dot` | **the graph** — graphviz DOT text (`text/plain`) | 20 | `/thy/trace/N/intdot/proof/[/...]` | `html` | wrapper page embedding `` | 21 | `/thy/trace/N/main/cases/{raw,refined}/i/j` | `json` | `{html,title}`; html contains `` | 22 23 The DOT text at `interactive-graph-def` is the whole deliverable. `intdot` is a 24 fixed HTML shell (only the `` and the `dotsrc` path vary). The graph is 25 rendered client-side from the DOT by `/static/js/intdot-graph.es.js`. 26 27 **JSON graph payload** [GAP]: the binary exposes a *separate* `--with-json` 28 rendering backend (`tamarin-prover interactive --help`). No JSON rendering tool 29 is installed here and no `interactive-graph-def` body in the corpus is JSON — 30 the entire corpus is the DOT backend. JSON graph format is therefore unobserved. 31 32 **Graph options** [RESOLVED §8]: the graph URL *does* take query parameters, but 33 they are cookie-driven and added by the UI JS — `simplification=N` (level, default 34 2, always sent), plus the flags `uncompact=`/`uncompress=` (sent at level 0), 35 `unabbreviate=`, `no-auto-sources=`, `clustering=true`. The earlier 36 "byte-identical" observation was because varying only `simplification=1/2/3` does 37 not change output on any probed graph (see §8). The real transforms are triggered 38 by the boolean flags. See §8. 39 40 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 41 ## 2. DOT document structure 42 43 Two headers occur, selected by a single rule (§4): 44 45 ### 2a. Simple header (non-clustered) 46 ``` 47 digraph "G" { 48 nodesep="0.3"; 49 ranksep="0.3"; 50 node[fontsize="8",fontname="Helvetica",width="0.3",height="0.2"]; 51 edge[fontsize="8",fontname="Helvetica"]; 52 <statements…> 53 54 } 55 ``` 56 57 ### 2b. Compact header (clustered) 58 ``` 59 digraph "G" { 60 nodesep="0.8"; 61 ranksep="0.8"; 62 sep="4"; 63 splines="true"; 64 overlap="false"; 65 pack="true"; 66 packmode="cluster"; 67 concentrate="true"; 68 compound="true"; 69 remincross="true"; 70 mclimit="10"; 71 nslimit="20"; 72 nslimit1="20"; 73 ordering="out"; 74 rankdir="TB"; 75 showboxes="false"; 76 clusterrank="local"; 77 node[fontsize="8",fontname="Helvetica",width="0.3",height="0.2",margin="0.05,0.05",shape="ellipse"]; 78 edge[fontsize="8",fontname="Helvetica",penwidth="1.5",arrowsize="0.5",color="black",style="solid",weight="8"]; 79 <statements…> 80 81 } 82 ``` 83 84 ### Block / whitespace rule (byte-exact) 85 Every block (`digraph … {`, `subgraph … {`, anonymous `{`) is emitted as: 86 `OPEN "\n"` then each inner statement as `STMT "\n"`, then one blank line, then 87 `}`. i.e. the body always ends `…\n\n}`. The top-level digraph is followed by a 88 final `\n`. Subgraph/anonymous blocks are themselves statements of their parent 89 (so the parent supplies the `\n` after their closing `}`). There are **no** blank 90 lines between sections (header attrs / nodes / edges / subgraphs run together); 91 the only blank line in each block is the one before its own `}`. 92 An empty graph is header attrs + blank + `}` (154 bytes). 93 94 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 95 ## 3. Node & edge syntax 96 97 Only three node shapes appear in proof graphs: `record`, `ellipse`, `plain`. 98 99 ### 3a. Record node (a rule / graph-node instance) 100 ``` 101 n<ID>[shape="record",label="{{<p> cell|<p> cell}|{<p> cell}|{<p> cell|<p> cell}}",fillcolor="#RRGGBB",style="filled",fontcolor="black|white",role="<Role>"]; 102 ``` 103 - Attribute order is fixed: `shape,label,fillcolor,style,fontcolor,role`. 104 - Label is a graphviz record: three top groups `{prem}|{info}|{concl}` (a group 105 may be absent/empty). Each group = cells joined by `|`. Each cell = 106 `<portid> text` (port id like `n0`, a space, then the fact text). Ports are 107 numbered per-graph; a record's own node id is allocated AFTER its port ids. 108 - Record-label text escaping: `<`→`\<`, `>`→`\>`, literal `{ } |`→`\{ \} \|`; 109 long facts are wrapped with `\l` (left-justified break) and indented with 110 ` ` runs. (The exact wrap width / term pretty-printing is **[GAP]** — it 111 is graphviz-layout / solver-term-renderer dependent; the model treats cell 112 text as pre-rendered.) 113 - `fillcolor` is a deterministic per-rule color (same rule name ⇒ same color 114 within a graph). `role="Undefined"` for plain multiset-rewriting rules; 115 otherwise the process/agent role. `fontcolor` is `black` on the light MSR 116 palette, `white` on the saturated per-role cluster palette. Exact color hash 117 is **[GAP]**; the model carries colors explicitly. 118 119 ### 3b. Ellipse node (atomic / knowledge / action / temporal node) 120 ``` 121 n<ID>[label="<text>",shape="ellipse"]; 122 n<ID>[label="<text>",shape="ellipse",color="<c>"]; 123 ``` 124 - Attribute order: `label,shape[,color]`. Optional `color` (`gray` for `!KU` 125 intruder-knowledge nodes, `darkblue` for action/event nodes, `gray30`, …). 126 - Ellipse text is NOT record-escaped (`<`,`>` appear literally, e.g. 127 `Session( 'I', <pk(~ltkA), pkB>, … )`). 128 129 ### 3c. Edge 130 ``` 131 n<S>[:<sport>] -> n<D>[:<dport>][<attrs>]; 132 ``` 133 Endpoints optionally carry a `:port` (record port). `<attrs>` is a 134 comma-separated attribute list, in observed key order. Frequent styles: 135 136 | attrs | meaning (inferred from context) | 137 |---|---| 138 | `color="red",style="dashed"` | intruder-deduction / `!KU` edge | 139 | `style="bold",weight="10.0",color="gray50"` | structural premise/conclusion edge | 140 | `style="bold",weight="10.0"` | structural edge (uncolored) | 141 | `color="gray30"` | message/std edge | 142 | `color="blue3",style="dashed"` | temporal-order edge | 143 | `color="orangered2"` | (deduction variant) | 144 | `color="black",style="dashed"` | before/less-than temporal edge | 145 | `style="invis"` | ranking edge to the legend node | 146 | `color="green",style="dotted"` / `purple`/`darkorange3` dashed | other goal edges | 147 148 ### 3d. Node shapes (§ Round-3 item 2) 149 Corpus census (all 12 022 dot + 81 manifests): only `record`, `ellipse`, `plain`. 150 **No trapezium appears in the crawl** — because the crawl captured only *proof* 151 graphs (solved states). Live probing (NAXOS_eCK, NSLPK3 case-distinction graphs) 152 adds one more shape: 153 - **`invtrapezium`** — a `(#var, idx)` placeholder for an **unresolved graph node** 154 referenced by a still-open premise, e.g. `n9[label="(#i, 0)",shape="invtrapezium"]`. 155 `#var` is the (temporal) node variable, `idx` the premise index; the node is fed 156 by a structural edge from the conclusion that must satisfy that premise 157 (`n5:n2 -> n9[style="bold",weight="10.0",color="gray50"]`). Appears in both the 158 compressed default and the uncompressed variant, in every source-case graph 159 tested. Attr order = ellipse's (`label,shape`). 160 - **`trapezium`** (spec-named dual) was **NOT observed** in any probe (all case 161 graphs of two theories, compressed + uncompressed) nor the corpus. Recorded as 162 unobserved; the serializer accepts any shape string so it can be emitted if a 163 caller supplies one. 164 165 ### 3e. Node-id / port allocation (§ Round-3 item 3) — byte-verified over 12 022 166 One global monotonic counter, ids handed out in **emission order** so file order == 167 id order. For each node in order: 168 - a **record** takes one id per cell (in cell order: premises, then the info cell, 169 then conclusions) and **then** one id for the node itself — so a `k`-cell record 170 occupies `n<p>…n<p+k-1>` (ports) and `n<p+k>` (node); 171 - every other kind (ellipse / plain / invtrapezium) takes exactly one id. 172 173 Example: a 4-prem/1-info/3-concl record takes ports `n0…n6`, node `n7`; the next 174 ellipse is `n8`. Verified 12022/12022 in `mine_ids.py` and again in Rust 175 (`tests/alloc_corpus.rs`, via `NodeIdAllocator`). 176 177 ### 3f. Record-cell term rendering + line wrapping (§ Round-3 item 1) 178 **Group structure** (byte-verified over 160 409 records): the label is 179 `{grp}|{grp}|{grp}` where the groups are the *non-empty* ones among 180 `[premises, info, conclusions]`, in that order; the **info** group is always 181 present (100 %). An empty premise or conclusion group is dropped (a source rule 182 renders `{info}|{concl}`). 183 - **Info cell**: `#<temporal> : <RuleName>` and, if the rule has action facts, 184 `[<action>, …]` (e.g. `#i : I_Complete[Complete( … ), …]`, or bare `#vf.5 : Fresh`). 185 - **Premise/conclusion cell**: a single fact. 186 - **Fact spacing**: a fact pads — `Name( a, b )`, and `Name( )` with no args; 187 a **function application** does not — `f(a, b)`; a **tuple** is `<a, b>`. Verified 188 against the corpus. 189 - **Escaping**: inside a record label, `< > { } |` each get a leading backslash; 190 everything else (quotes, `~ $ ^ * ⊕`, spaces) is literal. 191 - **Line wrapping** — the observable FORMAT is byte-exact; the break DECISION is a 192 GAP. When a group breaks, physical lines are separated by `\l` (with a trailing 193 `\l`), and each continuation line is indented with a ` ` run **equal to the 194 column of the broken group's first element** (just after `( ` for a fact, after 195 `<` for a tuple, after `[` for an action list). This alignment rule was verified 196 across 188 192 wrapped cells. **GAP:** *when* a break happens — the first physical 197 line length ranges 5..87 (not a flat width), so the break is the solver's 198 Wadler-style pretty-printer's width/group decision, not derivable from output. A 199 fact whose closing `)` breaks onto its own line at the functor column is a further 200 layout variant of that same gap. 201 202 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 203 ## 4. Clustering / simplification (§ priority 3) 204 205 **Trigger rule (byte-verified over all 14705 graphs, 0 violations):** 206 > The COMPACT clustered rendering (§2b, with `subgraph "cluster_…"` blocks) is 207 > used **iff the graph contains at least one node with `role` ≠ `"Undefined"`.** 208 > Otherwise the SIMPLE header (§2a) is used. 209 210 Non-Undefined roles arise from process/SAPIC agents (e.g. `Initiator`, 211 `Responder`, `server`, `Tag`, `v`). Pure multiset-rewriting theories emit the 212 simple header everywhere; role-annotated theories emit the compact header on 213 every non-empty graph (empty graphs have no role nodes ⇒ simple). 214 215 ### Cluster block 216 ``` 217 subgraph "cluster_<Role>_Session_<k>" { 218 nodesep="0.6"; 219 ranksep="0.6"; 220 label="<Role>_Session_<k>"; 221 style="filled"; 222 color="#RRGGBBAA"; 223 penwidth="2"; 224 fillcolor="#RRGGBBAA"; 225 overlap="false"; 226 sep="4"; 227 n<ID>[shape="record",label="…",fillcolor="#RRGGBB",style="filled",fontcolor="white",role="<Role>"]; 228 229 } 230 ``` 231 - Cluster name = `cluster_<clusterlabel>`; `label` = the clusterlabel 232 (`<Role>_Session_<k>`). Nodes grouped by (role, session instance). 233 - `color`/`fillcolor` are an 8-hex ARGB (alpha `4C`, ~30%) hashed from the 234 cluster; node `fillcolor` is the saturated per-role color. Exact hash **[GAP]**. 235 236 ### What "simplification" means here 237 There is no exposed simplification *level*; the corpus reflects one fixed 238 rendering pipeline. The observable "simplification" is the fixed compression the 239 server applies before rendering: intruder-knowledge (`!KU`) nodes are drawn as 240 gray ellipses; single-premise/conclusion structural chains are drawn with bold 241 weight-10 edges; role nodes are packed into per-session clusters (compact mode). 242 Reproducing the *content* of that compression from a raw constraint system 243 requires the solver and is **[GAP]** — out of scope for a rendering crate. 244 245 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 246 ## 5. Node abbreviation (§ priority 2) 247 248 When a graph is drawn, sufficiently complex sub-terms are replaced by short 249 NAMES and listed in a legend node. Observed from 113 924 legend rows. 250 251 ### 5a. Legend node 252 A `plain` node with an HTML-like label, placed in a sink-rank block: 253 ``` 254 { 255 rank="sink"; 256 n<ID>[shape="plain",label=<<TABLE BORDER="1" CELLBORDER="0" CELLSPACING="3" CELLPADDING="1"><TR>…row0…</TR> 257 <65 spaces><TR>…row1…</TR> 258 … 259 <65 spaces><TR>…rowN…</TR></TABLE>>]; 260 261 } 262 ``` 263 - Each row: 264 `<TR><TD ALIGN="LEFT" VALIGN="TOP"><FONT COLOR="#000000">NAME</FONT></TD> <TD ALIGN="LEFT" VALIGN="TOP">=</TD> <TD ALIGN="LEFT" VALIGN="TOP">EXPANSION</TD></TR>` 265 (single spaces between `</TD>` and the next `<TD>`). 266 - The first `<TR>` is inline after the `<TABLE …>` tag; every continuation `<TR>` 267 is on its own line indented by **65 spaces = the byte length of the 268 `<TABLE BORDER="1" CELLBORDER="0" CELLSPACING="3" CELLPADDING="1">` opening 269 tag** (graphviz hang-indent of the table's children; independent of node id). 270 - EXPANSION is HTML-escaped: `<`→`<`, `>`→`>`, `&`→`&`. Single quotes 271 and `* ^ ~ $ ⊕` are literal. 272 - The legend node is wired to the rest with `style="invis"` edges (from a couple 273 of real nodes to the legend), emitted AFTER the sink block. 274 275 ### 5b. Naming scheme (byte-verified rule) 276 `NAME = <PREFIX><n>`, n = 1,2,3,… per prefix. 277 `PREFIX` = the first **two alphabetic characters, uppercased**, of the ROOT 278 symbol's *name* (one letter if the name has only one). The root symbol name is: 279 - function application `f(…)` ⇒ `f` (`sign`→SI, `senc`→SE, `hash`→HA, 280 `h`/`h1`/`h2`→H, `KDF`/`kdf1`→KD, `pk`→PK, `aenc`/`aead`→AE, `pmult`→PM, …) 281 - public constant `'c'` ⇒ `c` (`'uninitialized'`→UN, `'F_status'`→FS, 282 `'ho_req_ack'`→HO, `'no_message_state'`→NO — non-letters skipped: F,s→FS) 283 - variable `~v` / `$v` / `v` ⇒ `v` (`~AMF_UE_NGAP_ID`→AM, `~cid_N26`→CI, 284 `~gNB_UE_ID`→GN, `~mid…`→MI, `commitmsg`→CO, `StateChannel`→ST) 285 - infix operator, mapped to its function name then the letter rule: 286 `^`→exp→**EX**, `*`→mult→**MU**, `++`→union→**UN**, `⊕`→xor→**XO**. 287 288 Abbreviations NEST: an inner abbreviated term is referenced by name inside an 289 outer expansion (`KD1='KDF(EX1)'`, `MA1='mac(EX1, SI1)'`, `EX1="'g'^MU1"`, 290 `H1="h(<…, EX2, EX3, EX1, …>)"`). So naming is applied bottom-up. 291 292 Per-prefix numbering is by tamarin's canonical term order, NOT by first 293 appearance in the drawing (observed: `In(EX2)` is the first occurrence yet gets 294 `EX2`, and larger/deeper terms get the lower numbers within a prefix). The exact 295 tie-break total order is **[GAP]** (needs the solver's term ordering); the crate 296 assigns per-prefix numbers over a defined order and matches whenever that order 297 is unambiguous. 298 299 ### 5c. Selection — WHICH sub-terms get abbreviated **[RESOLVED — see REPORT2.md]** 300 301 **Rule (three necessary gates; each confirmed by a controlled black-box probe):** 302 > A sub-term `t` is abbreviated **iff** 303 > 1. `renderLen(t) ≥ 10` (Unicode-scalar length of the fully-expanded surface 304 > rendering, *including* `'…'` quotes and `~`/`$` sigils), AND 305 > 2. `occ(t) ≥ 2` (occurrences over the whole constraint system — graph node 306 > facts **and** the sequent's goals/formulas — edge-shared messages count 307 > once), AND 308 > 3. `t` is **not** a tuple `<…>`. 309 > Naming is bottom-up: nested eligible sub-terms are named first 310 > (`LO1='longarg123'`, then `H1='h(LO1)'`). 311 312 This is a rendered **length** gate, not the *size* or *occurrence* the earlier 313 note guessed at — which is why it looked context-free-yet-unexplained. It 314 cleanly settles the old counter-observations: `sign(<…,'g'^~ekR>,~ltkI)` is ≥10 315 chars (abbreviated); `KDF(z)` is 6 chars (never abbreviated, whatever its count); 316 `mac(z,SI1)` and `'g'^~lkR` are below the length/occurrence thresholds in their 317 graphs. `'g'^~lkR` vs `'g'^~lkR.1` differ because the `.1` copy reaches occ 2 in 318 the system while the other does not. 319 320 **Corpus-exactness (necessary direction):** across all **97 538** legend 321 expansions in **11 564** graphs, the minimum rendered length is exactly **10** 322 (0 below) and **0** entries are top-level tuples. Among terms drawn ≥2× in a 323 graph, **0** abbreviated terms violate len≥10 ∧ ¬tuple. Abbreviated terms with 324 graph-occurrence 1 (7 800) all reach ≥2 once the sequent is counted. 325 326 **Controlled probes (crafted theories, live server; see QUERIES.log):** 327 - `'12345678'` (10 chars, ×2) abbreviated; `'1234567'` (9, ×2) not ⇒ boundary = 10. 328 - `'ABCDEFGH'`/`'onceonly12'` (×1) not abbreviated; `'twicelong1'` (×2) is; 329 a 42-char constant (×1) not abbreviated ⇒ occ ≥ 2 is a hard gate at any length. 330 - `<'aa','bb','cc'>` (18 chars, ×2) not abbreviated while sibling `h('longarg123')` 331 (×2) is ⇒ tuple exclusion + bottom-up nesting. 332 333 **Residual (sufficiency):** for **AC/DH-operator** sub-terms (`^ * ⊕ ++ inv em 334 hp pmult`) tamarin counts occurrences over the *normalised* (AC-flattened, 335 DH-normal-form) term, not the surface rendering, so the same term can be 336 abbreviated in one proof state and not another (`em(hp($A),hp($B))` inline in 337 state `56eeb42d`, `EM1` in `54eb2e2c`). 93 % of apparent exceptions are such 338 AC/DH cases; the rest are validation artifacts from expanding shared 339 abbreviations before counting. Outside AC/DH terms the rule is exact. 340 341 The crate `abbrev::select` implements this rule (`MIN_ABBREV_LEN=10`, 342 `MIN_ABBREV_OCC=2`, tuple-excluded, bottom-up); tests reproduce every probe. 343 Byte-parity of the selection set is claimed for non-AC/DH terms and gated by the 344 caller supplying the full-system term multiset. 345 346 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 347 ## 6. System → graph mapping (§ Round-3 item 4) 348 349 From paired `main/proof` sequents and `interactive-graph-def` graphs, the 350 structural mapping a proof state yields (each point traces to §3d/§3f and the 351 content census; the *content* — which nodes/edges exist — is a solver GAP): 352 - a **rule instance** `#t : Rule[actions]` → a `record` node with groups 353 `{premises}|{info}|{conclusions}` (empty prem/concl dropped, info always kept); 354 - an **intruder-knowledge fact** → a gray `!KU( m ) @ #t` ellipse; 355 - a **protocol action/event** → a darkblue `Fact @ #t` ellipse; 356 - a **compressed intruder rule** → an uncolored `#t : rule` ellipse (§4 compression); 357 - an **unresolved node** referenced by an open premise → an `invtrapezium` 358 `(#var, idx)` (§3d); 359 - **edges** connect conclusion-port → premise-port (structural bold w10 [gray50]), 360 intruder deduction (red dashed), message (gray30), temporal order 361 (blue3/black dashed), etc. — the finite §3c vocabulary; 362 - ids from §3e; header from §4 (role); legend from §5. 363 364 `graph-clean::generate` implements this over an independent input model 365 (`System`/`GraphNode`/`RuleInstance`/`SysEdge`), taking node/edge lists, colors, 366 and cell text as INPUTS. `tests/generate_tests.rs` reproduces a live source-case 367 graph (`nsl_invtrap.dot`) **byte-exact** end-to-end. 368 369 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 370 ## 7. Simplification / abbreviation options (§ Round-3 item 5) 371 372 The UI's "Graph simplification off/L1/L2/L3" menu and abbreviation toggle are 373 cookie-driven and appended by the JS as graph-URL **query params** (mined from 374 `/static/js/tamarin-prover-ui.js`, confirmed by live diffs — QUERIES.log): 375 376 | param | UI sends when | observed effect | 377 |--------------------|----------------------------------|------------------------------------------| 378 | `simplification=N` | always (N=level, default 2) | selects level; **N alone changed no probed graph** (L1≡L2≡L3) | 379 | `uncompact=` | level 0 only | node COMPACTION off (lone intruder rule → full record vs ellipse) | 380 | `uncompress=` | level 0 only | system COMPRESSION off (Fresh sources + intruder rules re-expand to records) | 381 | `unabbreviate=` | abbreviate cookie off | abbreviation off (terms inlined, legend block omitted) | 382 | `no-auto-sources=` | auto-sources cookie off | disables auto-source precomputation | 383 | `clustering=true` | clustering cookie on | forces role clustering | 384 385 The default (no params, as the corpus was crawled) = compressed + compacted + 386 abbreviated (≡ L1/L2/L3). `graph-clean::options::Options` models the knobs and 387 reproduces the exact query string; the abbreviation on/off transform is applied 388 directly (drop legend / inline). **GAPS:** the L1/L2/L3 distinction (unobserved on 389 every probed graph) and the *content* of compress/compact (a solver transform — 390 the caller supplies the already-(un)compressed node set). 391 392 -------------------------------------------------------------------------------- 393 ## 8. What the crate reproduces vs. gaps 394 395 Reproduced & byte-tested against captured/live payloads: 396 - DOT document assembly: both headers, block/whitespace rule, node lines 397 (record/ellipse/plain/**invtrapezium**), edge lines with ports & attrs, cluster 398 subgraphs, legend sink-block + invis edges, empty graph. Round-trip reproduces 399 **12 022/12 022** corpus payloads byte-exact (`tests/roundtrip.rs`). 400 - **Node-id/port allocation** (§3e): `NodeIdAllocator`; validated 12 022/12 022 via 401 the crate model (`tests/alloc_corpus.rs`). 402 - **Record-cell rendering** (§3f): fact/function/tuple spacing, info-cell shape, 403 group-drop rule, record escaping, and the wrap FORMAT (alignment indent + `\l`), 404 the last reproducing an observed wrapped cell byte-exact (`src/render.rs`). 405 - **System → graph GENERATION** (§6): `generate` over the independent `System` 406 model; reproduces a live source-case graph byte-exact (`tests/generate_tests.rs`). 407 - **Simplification/abbreviation options** (§7): `Options` query-string model. 408 - Abbreviation naming, numbering, legend HTML (65-space indent), and the SELECTION 409 rule (§5c, REPORT2.md), plus the cluster/compact trigger (§4). 410 411 Documented gaps (need the GPL solver or an unavailable backend): 412 - JSON graph backend format (unavailable / not in corpus). 413 - **Record-cell wrap DECISION** (§3f): the pretty-printer width/group that decides 414 *where* to break (first-line length 5..87, not a flat width). The FORMAT/indent 415 are exact; the decision is not derivable from output. 416 - **L1/L2/L3 distinction** (§7) and the **compress/compact content** (§4, §6): 417 which nodes/edges a raw constraint system yields — a solver transform. 418 - Per-rule/per-cluster **color hashes** (§3a, §4); the `trapezium` dual (§3d, unobserved). 419 - Abbreviation of AC/DH sub-terms (§5c residual, normalised-form occurrence). 420 - Canonical per-prefix numbering tie-break (§5b); empty-prefix start index. 421

1	# REPORT2 — the abbreviation SELECTION rule (BEHAVIOR.md §5c gap)
2	
3	Clean-room continuation of `graphdot`. Everything below traces to observed
4	oracle output: the captured DOT corpus (`oracle/dot_corpus/`, 12 022 unique
5	payloads), the captured crawl manifests (`oracle/captured_responses/`, 81
6	theories; the `main/proof` json giving the full sequent paired 1:1 with each
7	`interactive-graph-def` DOT), and controlled probes of the black-box server
8	(`hs_server` binary, `--port=` form). No tamarin-prover source was read.
9	
10	## Result in one line
11	
12	> A sub-term `t` of a constraint system is abbreviated in the graph **iff**
13	> (1) its rendered length >= **10**, (2) it occurs >= **2** times in the system,
14	> and (3) it is not a tuple `<...>`. Naming is bottom-up (nested eligible
15	> sub-terms are named first).
16	
17	The prior named puzzle -- why `sign(...)` at 3 occurrences is abbreviated while
18	`KDF(z)` at 4 is not -- is resolved: the gate is rendered **string length**, not
19	structural size or occurrence count. `sign(<...>,...)` is >=10 chars; `KDF(z)`
20	is 6. The earlier investigator measured *size* and *occurrence* and so could not
21	separate them.
22	
23	## Evidence
24	
25	### (1) Length >= 10 -- necessary, corpus-exact
26	Over **all 97 538** legend expansions in **11 564** graphs (every legend in the
27	corpus), the minimum fully-expanded rendered length is **exactly 10**; **zero**
28	are shorter. Histogram floor: len10=7397, len11=3071, len12=3776, ... nothing at
29	5-9. Length is measured on tamarin's own rendering *including* surface
30	decorations -- quotes, `~`/`$` sigils (a bare `pk(PL1)` is only 7 chars but is
31	abbreviated because it *expands* to >=10; the decision is on the full expansion).
32	
33	Controlled probe (`LenTest.spthy`): a rule whose one node emits
34	`Keep(~x, '12345678', '12345678', '1234567', '1234567')`. Observed graph:
35	`Keep( ~x, 2, 2, '1234567', '1234567' )` with legend `2 = '12345678'`.
36	`'12345678'` = 10 chars -> abbreviated; `'1234567'` = 9 chars, **same occurrence
37	count** -> not. The boundary is exactly 10.
38	
39	### (2) Occurrence >= 2 -- necessary, hard gate independent of length
40	Structural per-graph analysis of 5 725 graphs (156 602 distinct candidate
41	sub-terms): among terms drawn >=2 times, **every** abbreviated term satisfies
42	len>=10 AND not-tuple -- **0** counterexamples. The 7 800 abbreviated terms the
43	graph-only occurrence "misses" all have graph-occurrence 1: they occur once in
44	the *drawn* graph but >=2 in the full system (the sequent's goals/formulas). This
45	is why terms such as `SI3 = sign(~y, k)` appear in a legend while being drawn
46	**nowhere** in the graph -- the abbreviation universe is the whole constraint
47	system, not the drawn nodes. Pairing each DOT with its `main/proof` json sequent
48	recovers those second occurrences.
49	
50	Controlled probe (`OccTest.spthy`): `'ABCDEFGH'` (10 chars, x1) and
51	`'onceonly12'` (12 chars, x1) are **not** abbreviated; `'twicelong1'` (12, x2)
52	**is** (`TW1`). Probe (`LongOnce.spthy`): a **42-char** constant appearing once
53	is **not** abbreviated, while a 12-char constant appearing twice is. So there is
54	no "very long => abbreviate at 1 occurrence" branch -- occ >= 2 is a hard gate.
55	
56	### (3) Tuples are never abbreviated -- necessary, corpus-exact
57	**0 of 97 538** legend expansions is a top-level tuple. Only function
58	applications, exponentiations, AC-operator terms (`* (+) ++`), and atoms (long
59	constants/variables) are abbreviated. Controlled probe (`TupTest.spthy`):
60	`<'aa','bb','cc'>` (18 chars, x2) stays inline, while the sibling
61	`h('longarg123')` (x2) is abbreviated to `H1` with `LO1 = 'longarg123'`,
62	`H1 = h(LO1)` -- confirming the tuple exclusion and **bottom-up nesting** (inner
63	`'longarg123'` named before the enclosing `h(...)`), and the prefix rule
64	(`lo`->LO, `h`->H).
65	
66	## Accuracy and the residual
67	
68	The three gates are **necessary** with 0 corpus counterexamples (length, tuple)
69	/ 0 counterexamples among drawn->=2 terms (occurrence). For **sufficiency** --
70	is every (len>=10 AND occ>=2 AND not-tuple) term abbreviated? -- the controlled
71	probes say yes for ordinary terms, and the corpus agrees except for one
72	characterized residual:
73	
74	* **93.3%** of apparent sufficiency-exceptions occur in graphs using an **AC/DH
75	  operator** (`^ * (+) ++ inv em hp pmult`). Example: in state `56eeb42d`,
76	  `em(hp($A), hp($B))` (18 chars, drawn 7x) is *not* abbreviated, yet in a later
77	  state `54eb2e2c` the *same* term *is* (`EM1`). The difference is how DH
78	  exponentiation groups the term: tamarin counts occurrences over the
79	  **normalised** (AC-flattened, DH-normal-form) term, which a surface count does
80	  not model. This is solver-normalisation-dependent and cannot be derived from
81	  the rendering alone.
82	
83	* The remaining **~7%** are terms nested inside a *shared* abbreviated term
84	  (e.g. `pk(~ska.3)` inside one `sign(...)` threaded through an edge and drawn as
85	  `SI` in two facts). These are **measurement artifacts** of validating by
86	  *expanding* abbreviations before counting: expansion turns one edge-shared
87	  occurrence into several. The occurrence gate counts occurrences in the actual
88	  constraint system (an edge-shared message counts once), so these are not true
89	  rule violations -- a direct search finds **no** graph where such a term appears
90	  literally >=2x and is left un-abbreviated.
91	
92	Net: outside AC/DH-normalised sub-terms the rule is exact (confirmed by
93	controlled probe and by the AC-free portion of the corpus); the one honest gap
94	is the AC/DH occurrence-over-normal-form count.
95	
96	## What was implemented (`graph-clean`)
97	
98	`abbrev::select(roots) -> Vec<Term>` implements the rule: enumerate every
99	non-tuple sub-term of `roots` with `render_len() >= 10`, keep those occurring
100	>= 2 times, return them shortest-first (bottom-up). Thresholds are exposed as
101	`MIN_ABBREV_LEN=10`, `MIN_ABBREV_OCC=2` and via `select_with(roots,min_len,
102	min_occ)` for probing. `roots` must be the full multiset of constraint-system
103	terms (graph node-fact arguments **and** sequent terms) because occurrence is a
104	whole-system count. Feeding the result through the existing `Abbreviator`
105	reproduces the legend end-to-end.
106	
107	`term::Term` gained `is_tuple()` and `render_len()` (Unicode-scalar count of the
108	full rendering, matching the observed 10-char boundary).
109	
110	Tests (`tests/abbrev_tests.rs`, all green) reproduce each controlled probe:
111	`select_length_boundary_is_ten`,
112	`select_requires_two_occurrences_even_for_long_terms`,
113	`select_never_abbreviates_a_tuple`, `select_then_name_reproduces_probe_legend`,
114	`select_counts_nested_occurrences`, `select_with_custom_thresholds`. Full suite
115	is 24 tests incl. the 12 022-payload byte-exact roundtrip.
116	
117	## Minor open detail
118	A numeric-only constant (`'12345678'`, no alphabetic chars) gets an **empty
119	prefix**, so its abbreviation name is a bare number -- observed as `2` for the
120	first such entry. The starting index for empty-prefix names was not pinned down
121	(single observation); it does not affect the length/occurrence/tuple rule.
122

#!/usr/bin/env bash

esac

content.text:
Critical result: `simplification=0..3` alone — via URL param, cookie, or both — is byte-identical to baseline on this rich 17-node graph. The graph changes **only** when `uncompact=&uncompress=` flags are present (7792 vs 5668 bytes), and that change is identical with or without a cookie. So the server honors the **URL param** directly (a change demonstrably took effect), and there is no session-state override. The level *number* does nothing by itself.

import sys, re

def cells_of(dotfile):
    txt = open(dotfile, encoding='utf-8').read()
    # find the Out( ... ) cell inside a record label
    m = re.search(r'label="\{.*?\}"', txt, re.S)
    return txt

def analyze_cell(cell):
    # cell is the raw record-label text for one fact (may contain \l and  )
    parts = cell.split(r'\l')
    out = []
    for i, p in enumerate(parts):
        m = re.match(r'((?: )*)', p)
        indent = len(m.group(1)) // 6
        disp = p.replace(' ', '')
        disp = disp.replace(r'\<', '<').replace(r'\>', '>').replace(r'\{','{').replace(r'\}','}').replace(r'\|','|')
        out.append((i, indent, len(disp), disp))
    return out

def extract_out(dotfile):
    txt = open(dotfile, encoding='utf-8').read()
    # Out( ... ) up to the closing that precedes | or }
    m = re.search(r'(Out\( .*?\\> \))', txt, re.S)
    if not m:
        m = re.search(r'(Out\( .*?\))(?=[|}])', txt, re.S)
    return m.group(1) if m else None

if __name__ == '__main__':
    for f in sys.argv[1:]:
        cell = extract_out(f)
        print("==", f, "==")
        if cell is None:
            print("  (no Out cell found)")
            continue
        for i, indent, ln, disp in analyze_cell(cell):
            # column where this line's content ends (indent + len)
            print(f"  line{i}: indent={indent} textlen={ln} endcol={indent+ln} :: {disp}")

import os, sys

TMPL = '''theory {name} begin

rule Emit:
  [ Fr(~x) ] --[ Go(~x) ]-> [ Out({arg}) ]

lemma go: exists-trace "Ex x #i. Go(x)@i"

end
'''

def write(path, name, arg):
    with open(path, 'w') as f:
        f.write(TMPL.format(name=name, arg=arg))

if __name__ == '__main__':
    outdir = sys.argv[1]
    os.makedirs(outdir, exist_ok=True)
    mode = sys.argv[2]
    if mode == 'atom2':
        # two-element tuple: first a public constant of P a's, second 'y'
        for P in range(60, 96):
            arg = "<'" + ('a'*P) + "', 'y'>"
            write(f"{outdir}/s{P}.spthy", f"S{P}", arg)
        print("atom2:", 60, "..", 95)
    elif mode == 'nelem':
        # N distinct short constants in a tuple
        for N in range(1, 30):
            elts = ", ".join("'a%02d'" % k for k in range(1, N+1))
            arg = "<" + elts + ">"
            write(f"{outdir}/n{N}.spthy", f"N{N}", arg)
        print("nelem:", 1, "..", 29)

import json, sys

# Load both sweeps and, for every broken line, compute the absolute end column
# of the last token on the line and the would-be end column had the next token
# stayed. Deduce W = max fit end-col, and min break-forcing end-col.

def endcols(results):
    fits = []    # end col of content that stayed on a line (upper bound examples)
    for name, lines in results.items():
        for (indent, ln, disp) in lines:
            # strip trailing record noise '}' on the very last physical line
            end = indent + ln
            fits.append((name, indent, ln, end, disp))
    return fits

for path in sys.argv[1:]:
    R = json.load(open(path))
    print("==", path, "==")
    rows = endcols(R)
    # Only look at lines that are 'content' (not the lone ) or } peels)
    content = [r for r in rows if r[2] > 3]
    mx = max(content, key=lambda r: r[3])
    print("  max content end-col:", mx[3], "in", mx[0], "line:", repr(mx[4][:40]))

def phys_lines(cell):

    parts = cell.split(r'\l')
    out = []
    for p in parts:
        m = re.match(r'((?: )*)', p)
        indent = len(m.group(1)) // 6
        disp = p.replace(' ', '').replace(r'\<', '<').replace(r'\>', '>')
        out.append((indent, disp))
    while out and out[-1][1].strip('}') == '':
        out.pop()
    return out

def show(tag):
    cell = fx[tag]
    print(f"--- {tag} ---")

def simulate(nelem, width, lookahead):
    els = ["'a%02d'" % k for k in range(1, nelem+1)]
    prefix_open_col = len("Out( <")   # 6
    cont_indent = 6
    lines = []            # list of (indent, text)
    col = prefix_open_col
    cur = "Out( <"
    first = True
    for i, e in enumerate(els):
        last = (i == nelem-1)
        piece = e if (col==prefix_open_col and first and False) else e
        # separator handling: comma attaches to element unless last
        tok = e + ("" if last else ",")
        need = len(tok)
        # lookahead: if not last, also require room for the following separator space + next token start? emulate Wadler: require tok + next element flat fit
        test_end = col + (0 if (cur.endswith("<") ) else 1) + need

            la_end = end + 1 + len(nxt)
            la_ok = la_end <= width
        if first:
            cur += tok
            col = end
            first = False
        elif end <= width and (la_ok):
            cur += (" " if lead else "") + tok
            col = end
        else:
            lines.append(cur)
            cur = "&"*cont_indent + tok  # placeholder indent marker

    cur += ">"
    lines.append(cur)

import sys
sys.setrecursionlimit(100000)

# Wadler "A prettier printer" algebra.
# Doc = NIL | TEXT s Doc | LINE i Doc  (in the 'best' normal form we build via constructors)
# We use the classic representation with UNION for group.

NIL=('nil',)
def TEXT(s): return ('text', s)
def LINE(): return ('line',)          # breakable: space when flat, newline when broken
def SOFTBREAK(): return ('sbreak',)   # nothing when flat, newline when broken
def NEST(i,d): return ('nest', i, d)
def CAT(a,b): return ('cat', a, b)
def GROUP(d): return ('group', d)

def cats(ds):
    r=NIL
    for d in reversed(ds):
        r=CAT(d,r)
    return r

def flatten(d):
    t=d[0]
    if t=='nil': return d
    if t=='text': return d
    if t=='line': return TEXT(' ')
    if t=='sbreak': return NIL
    if t=='nest': return NEST(d[1], flatten(d[2]))
    if t=='cat': return CAT(flatten(d[1]), flatten(d[2]))
    if t=='group': return flatten(d[1])
    raise ValueError(t)

# best: layout algorithm. w=width, k=current column. docs=list of (indent, doc)
def fits(w, x):
    # x is a list of (i, doc) commands; check the remaining width w >=0 along first line
    while True:
        if w<0: return False
        if not x: return True
        i,d=x[0]; rest=x[1:]
        t=d[0]
        if t=='nil': x=rest
        elif t=='text': w-=len(d[1]); x=rest
        elif t=='cat': x=[(i,d[1]),(i,d[2])]+rest
        elif t=='nest': x=[(i+d[1],d[2])]+rest
        elif t=='line': return True   # a newline: first line ended, fits
        elif t=='sbreak': return True
        elif t=='group': x=[(i,flatten(d[1]))]+rest
        else: raise ValueError(t)

def best(w, k, cmds):
    out=[]
    x=list(cmds)
    while x:
        i,d=x.pop(0)
        t=d[0]
        if t=='nil': pass
        elif t=='text': out.append(d[1]); k+=len(d[1])
        elif t=='cat': x=[(i,d[1]),(i,d[2])]+x
        elif t=='nest': x=[(i+d[1],d[2])]+x
        elif t=='line': out.append('\n'+' '*i); k=i
        elif t=='sbreak': out.append('\n'+' '*i); k=i
        elif t=='group':
            flat=[(i,flatten(d[1]))]+x
            if fits(w-k, flat):
                x=flat
            else:
                x=[(i,d[1])]+x
        else: raise ValueError(t)
    return ''.join(out)

# Wadler fill: elements joined by a separator that is comma(non-break)+breakable-space,
# using the union so each break is decided with one-element lookahead.
def fill_tuple(elems):
    # returns a Doc for  e1, e2, ... eN  with fill behavior (no < > )
    # fill (x:y:zs) = group( flatten x <sep-flat> fill(y:zs)_flat ) ???
    # Use Wadler's fill directly:
    def go(es):
        if not es: return NIL
        if len(es)==1: return es[0]
        x=es[0]; y=es[1]; zs=es[2:]
        # x , <space|newline> rest
        # union: (flatten x + ", " + fill(flatten y : zs))  |  (x + ",\n" + fill(y:zs))
        flatrest = CAT(CAT(flatten(x), TEXT(', ')), go([flatten(y)]+zs))
        breakrest= CAT(CAT(x, TEXT(',')), CAT(SOFTLINE_NL(), go([y]+zs)))
        return UNION(flatrest, breakrest)
    return go(elems)

def SOFTLINE_NL(): return ('sbreak',)  # newline in broken branch
def UNION(a,b): return ('union', a, b)

# best must handle union
def best2(w, k, cmds):
    out=[]
    x=list(cmds)
    while x:
        i,d=x.pop(0)
        t=d[0]
        if t=='nil': pass
        elif t=='text': out.append(d[1]); k+=len(d[1])
        elif t=='cat': x=[(i,d[1]),(i,d[2])]+x
        elif t=='nest': x=[(i+d[1],d[2])]+x
        elif t=='line': out.append('\n'+' '*i); k=i
        elif t=='sbreak': out.append('\n'+' '*i); k=i
        elif t=='group':
            flat=[(i,flatten(d[1]))]+x
            if fits2(w-k, flat): x=flat
            else: x=[(i,d[1])]+x
        elif t=='union':
            a=[(i,d[1])]+x
            if fits2(w-k, a): x=a
            else: x=[(i,d[2])]+x
        else: raise ValueError(t)
    return ''.join(out)

def fits2(w, x):
    while True:
        if w<0: return False
        if not x: return True
        i,d=x[0]; rest=x[1:]
        t=d[0]
        if t=='nil': x=rest
        elif t=='text': w-=len(d[1]); x=rest
        elif t=='cat': x=[(i,d[1]),(i,d[2])]+rest
        elif t=='nest': x=[(i+d[1],d[2])]+rest
        elif t=='line': return True
        elif t=='sbreak': return True
        elif t=='group': x=[(i,flatten(d[1]))]+rest
        elif t=='union': x=[(i,d[1])]+rest   # fits checks the flat/first branch
        else: raise ValueError(t)

def render_fact_tuple(nelem, width):
    els=[TEXT("'a%02d'"%k) for k in range(1,nelem+1)]
    inner=fill_tuple(els)
    # tuple: < inner >   ; the < and > hug; use nest so continuation aligns under first element
    tup = CAT(TEXT('<'), CAT(NEST(0, inner), TEXT('>')))
    # fact Out( tuple )  with padding and the closing ) able to break to col 0
    # model:  "Out(" <> nest? " " <> tuple <> softbreak-to-0? ") "
    doc = CAT(TEXT('Out( '), CAT(NEST(6, tup), TEXT(' )')))
    # We render at column 0.
    s=best2(width, 0, [(0, doc)])
    return s

for n in [11,12,23,24]:

### 7a. The L1/L2/L3 distinction — RESOLVED (channel-verified negative, Session 4)

The Round-3 GAP ("simplification 1/2/3 produced byte-identical graphs") is **not a
probing artifact and not a hidden session channel** — it is the genuine server
behavior: **there is no L1/L2/L3 distinction; the `simplification=N` *number* is
inert for every N.** Established under the session-state lens the follow-up
demanded:

- **Channel.** The served JS (`tamarin-prover-ui.js`, lines 78–100 / 476–500)
  reads a *client-side* `simplification` cookie (default 2) and converts it into
  the graph-URL query param; at level 0 it *additionally* appends `uncompact=` +
  `uncompress=`. Levels 1/2/3 send **only** `simplification=N`. The server emits
  **no `Set-Cookie` on any response** (root, case-graph, main/cases json, proof
  method) — there is *no server-side session state*; the param is the only channel.
- **Change verified before diffing.** Driving the *flags* through the URL param
  demonstrably changes the graph (e.g. rich NSLPK3 case `refined/6/5`: default
  5 668 B → `uncompact=&uncompress=` 7 792 B), and the result is byte-identical
  whether or not a matching cookie is also sent. So the URL param **is** honored.
- **The number is inert.** On that 17-node case graph and on a **39-node, 27 KB**
  DH/bilinear proof-state graph (`eurosp19-eccDAA` analysed,
  `proof/functional_correctness/_`), every probe of `simplification` ∈
  {0,1,2,3,4,5,10,99} — via URL param, via cookie, and via both together, and
  crossed with every flag combination — collapses to a **single** md5. `flags_L0`
  … `flags_L99` are byte-identical. (Second theory NAXOS_eCK case `refined/4/1`

- **Line wrapping FORMAT** (byte-exact). When a group breaks, physical lines are
  separated by `\l` (with a trailing `\l`), and each continuation line is indented
  with a ` ` run **equal to the column of the broken group's first element**
  (just after `( ` for a fact, after `<` for a tuple, after `[` for an action
  list). Verified across 188 192 wrapped cells.
- **Line wrapping DECISION — RESOLVED (Session 4): a fixed-width paragraph fill,
  width = 87 columns, measured per fact from column 0.** The Round-3 claim ("first
  line 5..87, not a flat width, not derivable") is **refuted**: it *is* a flat
  width. Established by driving crafted single-node theories
  (`Out(<'a01', …, 'aN'>)`, `Out(<'aa…a', 'y'>)`) through the live server and
  sweeping the term width one column at a time:
  - **W = 87 absolute, functor-invariant.** A fact stays on one line iff its flat
    rendering is ≤ 87 columns; at 88 it breaks. Tested with functor names of length
    2, 3, 6, 10 (`Ba`,`Out`,`Fact12`,`Factabcdef`): the boundary was **always** at
    flat width 87 fits / 88 breaks, so the budget is counted from the functor's own
    column 0, *including* the functor name (a longer name simply fits fewer args).
    Corpus-consistent: 99.2 % of 190 044 physical lines have fact-content ≤ 87; the
    residual are single unbreakable atoms wider than 87 (which overflow verbatim).
  - **Greedy paragraph fill.** When a group overflows, elements pack left-to-right;
    the separator `, ` trails the element it follows and stays on the line, and the
    next element wraps when it would pass column 87. Continuation lines use the
    §3f indent. Example `Out(<'a01'…'a12'>)` (flat 91): line 0 =
    `Out( <'a01', … 'a11', ` (eleven elements + trailing `, `, ending col 83),
    `'a12'>` on the next line, then the fact's `)` on its own line.
  - **Delimiter peel.** The fact's closing `)` peels onto its own physical line at
    the **functor column** (col 0) whenever the fact breaks; a tuple's `>` peels to
    the **`<` column** when the last element fills the line
    (`Out( <'aa…(74)', 'y'` │ `>` │ `)`); an unbreakable atom wider than the budget
    overflows and only the trailing delimiters wrap.
  - **KNOWN RESIDUAL** (`fsep` lookahead): the underlying combinator's one-element
    lookahead lets a *continuation* line hold **one more** element than the first
    line at the same start column (first line 11, continuation 12 for the 5-col
    `'aNN'` elements). The width (87), the top-level fit, the first-line packing and
    the peel columns are byte-verified; reproducing the ±1 continuation lookahead
    and the boundary peel byte-for-byte would require the exact `fsep`/`nest` doc
    tree. `graph-clean::render` implements `FILL_WIDTH=87`, `fits_one_line`, and
    `paragraph_fill` (first-line-exact), tested against these captured probes.
